data.title: Americold anticipates occupancy pressures and lowers AFFO guidance to $1.39-$1.45 amid ongoing demand headwinds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Americold Realty Trust, Inc. (COLD) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO George F. Chappelle reported that Q2 unfolded as expected, closely mirroring Q1 and confirmed that "same-store economic occupancy declined slightly in the second quarter versus the first quarter of the year" due to typical seasonality and persistent demand headwinds. Chappelle highlighted that "rent and storage revenue from fixed commit contracts came in at 60% for the quarter, reflecting the quality of our mission-critical assets and the value we deliver to the customers who occupy them." Two new retail wins in Europe were cited as part of the company's global expansion in the retail and QSR business.
  • Chappelle emphasized continued improvements in labor productivity, stating that "our perm-to-temp hours ratio was 75-25," supporting operational flexibility and margin growth. Same-store warehouse services margins improved by 90 basis points year-over-year to 13.3%.
  • The company completed several key projects in Q2, including flagship developments in Kansas City, Allentown, and Dubai, all under budget. These projects are intended to leverage scale and partnerships for innovative market solutions.
  • Chappelle noted that AFFO per share for Q2 was $0.36 and warned of ongoing pressure from "interest rates, tariffs, inflation, government benefit reductions and excess capacity" leading to a more conservative outlook for the remainder of the year.
  • CFO Jay E. Wells stated, "As a result of these continued market headwinds, we are reducing our AFFO guidance to $1.39 to $1.45 per share." Wells added, "Total net debt outstanding at the end of the quarter was $3.9 billion, with total liquidity of approximately $937 million. Net debt to pro forma core EBITDA was approximately 6.3x."

Outlook

  • The company now expects same-store economic occupancy levels for the year to decrease by approximately 250 to 450 basis points and same-store throughput to decrease by 1% to 4%. Sequentially, throughput is anticipated to lift slightly from Q2 to Q3, with modest occupancy gains in Q4.
  • Americold's revised AFFO guidance stands at $1.39 to $1.45 per share, as stated by CFO Wells, who explained that the guidance reduction reflects the absence of a traditional seasonal inventory build and persistent demand challenges.

Financial Results

  • AFFO per share for Q2 was $0.36, as reported by both Chappelle and Wells.
  • The company successfully executed several development projects under budget, including the Allentown expansion, which was completed at $79 million, and the Kansas City facility at $100 million.
  • Three planned exits of idled facilities were completed for total cash proceeds of $20 million, and a minority ownership interest in the SuperFrio joint venture in Brazil was exited for approximately $28 million in cash proceeds.
  • Total net debt outstanding at quarter end was $3.9 billion, with liquidity of $937 million.

Q&A

  • Samir Upadhyay Khanal, BofA Securities: Asked about pricing competitiveness and pressure. Chappelle responded, "the storage market remains very, very competitive when it comes to pricing, and we're even still seeing some moves into irrational... So we consider it to be under a significant amount of pressure. We expect it to remain under pressure for the balance of the year and quite frankly, until occupancy growth returns."
  • Khanal followed up regarding fixed commit contracts. Chambers explained these are "structured as multiyear arrangements... generally pegged at the peak amount of space that a customer is going to need for the year," with no annual volume resets and multiyear duration.
  • Stephen Thomas Sakwa, Evercore ISI: Probed on revenue pressure in the back half of the year. Chappelle attributed this to "pricing pressure" and "very unique situation when it comes to demand" involving "five or six headwinds" such as interest rates, tariffs, inflation, and excess capacity. Wells clarified, "we have removed any seasonality except for certain harvests that are guaranteed from our forecast."
  • Greg Michael McGinniss, Scotiabank: Asked about occupancy expectations and macro drivers. Chappelle confirmed, "we don't expect any seasonality in the second half" and listed several macro headwinds, describing them as "transitory in nature" but emphasizing that timing for improvement remains uncertain.
  • Todd Michael Thomas, KeyBanc: Inquired about customer use of internal cold storage capacity. Chappelle described it as a "relatively minor factor," noting customers are maximizing internal assets but expects this to reverse when demand improves.
  • Craig Allen Mailman, Citi: Asked about catalysts for inventory restocking. Chappelle said, "we need to see some of [the headwinds] improvable before we can reliably say that occupancy will improve."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was skeptical and focused on understanding the severity and duration of demand and pricing headwinds, with multiple questions on revenue pressure, occupancy trends, and customer contract structure.
  • Management maintained a neutral-to-slightly defensive stance, frequently referencing external macroeconomic headwinds and reiterating their focus on cost control, innovation, and international opportunities. Phrases such as "we believe" and "we are confident" were used, though caution was emphasized around forecasting improvements.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management conveyed increased caution and a more measured outlook, with fewer references to upside or normal seasonality.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for AFFO per share was reduced from $1.42-$1.52 in Q1 to $1.39-$1.45 in Q2, reflecting management's more conservative market view.
  • The company is no longer forecasting a seasonal inventory build for the second half, a shift from prior expectations.
  • The tone shifted from cautious optimism in Q1 to a more guarded and conservative stance in Q2.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on demand drivers, pricing, occupancy, and contract structure, but questions in Q2 were more persistent regarding the lack of seasonal uplift and potential for further downside.
  • The strategic focus has intensified on cost controls, international expansion, and fixed commitment contracts.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing headwinds from interest rates, tariffs, inflation, government benefit reductions, and excess capacity, all weighing on demand and occupancy.
  • Pricing pressure in the U.S. market was described as "intensifying," with some competitors engaging in "irrational" moves.
  • Customers are maximizing their own infrastructure, leading to slower third-party occupancy gains.
  • Management reiterated their focus on cost control and international growth, but noted the timing of demand recovery remains highly uncertain.

Final Takeaway

Americold Realty Trust enters the second half of 2025 with a more conservative outlook, lowering AFFO guidance and removing expectations for a seasonal inventory build due to persistent demand and pricing headwinds. While the company continues to execute on operational priorities, expand internationally, and manage costs, management stresses that multiple macroeconomic challenges are converging, and improvement in occupancy will depend on the easing of these pressures. Strategic investments in customer service, development, and fixed commitment contracts position Americold to capture growth when conditions improve, but near-term performance will be closely tied to the broader economic environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Hut 8 unexpectedly flips to GAAP profit in Q2 on digital asset gains
data.description:
Hut 8 Corp. (NASDAQ:HUT) on Thursday delivered GAAP profitability for the second quarter as "we delivered strong revenue and margin performance while advancing a fundamental shift in our asset commercialization profile," said CEO Asher Genoot.

Q2 GAAP EPS of $1.18, blowing past the -$0.15 consensus, jumped from -$1.30 in the previous quarter and -$0.78 a year earlier.

Revenue of $41.3M in Q2, trailing the $49.8M expected, accelerated from $21.8M in Q1 and $35.2M in Q2 2024.

HUT shares dipped 2.3% in late morning trading.

The company's Power unit generated $5.5M in revenue during the three-month period from Power Generation and Managed Services, while its Digital Infrastructure business raked in $1.5M from ASIC Colocation and CPU Colocation services. Compute revenue was $34.3M.

Total cost of revenue was $21.8M, up from $18.7M in the prior three-month period and $20.6M in last year's Q2.

Hut 8 (NASDAQ:HUT) held 10,667 bitcoin (BTC-USD) in reserve with a market value of $1.1B at June 30, 2025. That's up from 10,264 tokens at March 31, 2025, and 9,102 BTC at June 30, 2024.

Q2 earnings were bolstered by gains on digital assets of $217.6M vs. losses on digital assets of $71.8M a year earlier.

Adjusted EBITDA surged to $221.2M from -$117.7M in Q1 and -$57.5M in the year-ago period.

data.title: Papa John's raises international sales outlook to 2%-4% as transformation gains traction
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Papa John's International, Inc. (PZZA) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Todd Allan Penegor, President and CEO, emphasized the company's return to positive comparable sales and transaction growth in North America, citing, "With our barbell strategy and enhanced loyalty program in place, our brand strength has improved significantly." He highlighted the launch of Cheddar Crust in North America, the long-term menu addition of Shaq-a-Roni pizza, and strong value offerings such as the $6.99 Papa Pairings, which increased both average number of pies per order and pizza sales versus last year.
  • Internationally, Penegor pointed to the successful introduction of the Croissant Pizza in Dubai as an example of blending global food culture with Papa John's craftsmanship, stating, "We look forward to expanding the launch of this exciting product to other international markets."
  • Penegor outlined five strategic priorities: core product innovation, amplified marketing, technology investment, differentiated customer experience, and evolving the franchisee base. He also announced a signed agreement to sell the company’s ownership stake in a joint venture operating 85 restaurants, with proceeds earmarked for strategic investments and debt reduction.
  • Ravi Thanawala, EVP International & CFO, reported, "In the second quarter, global system-wide restaurant sales were $1.26 billion, up 4% in constant currency." Thanawala noted positive North America comparable sales of 1% and international comparable sales growth of 4%.

Outlook

  • The company raised its 2025 international comparable sales guidance to a range of 2% to 4%, while reiterating system-wide sales growth expectations of 2% to 5% and North America comparable sales guidance of flat to up 2% for the year.
  • Thanawala stated, "Through the first 5 weeks of the third quarter, North America comparable sales are down approximately 1% as we've seen a more cautious consumer and softer carryout business," but expects an acceleration through Q3 and further in Q4 as new product innovations launch.
  • The sale of the joint venture is expected to reduce fourth quarter consolidated revenue by approximately $15 million and annualized consolidated revenue by approximately $60 million, with negligible impact on net income.
  • Consolidated adjusted EBITDA guidance for 2025 remains at $200 million to $220 million.

Financial Results

  • Total revenues for Q2 were $529 million, an increase of 4%, driven by higher commissary revenues and partially offset by lower company-owned restaurant revenues due to U.K. refranchising and market optimization.
  • Commissary revenues rose by $20 million, while company-owned restaurant revenues declined by $6 million, reflecting the net closure and refranchising of restaurants in the U.K. and a $3 million domestic increase from comparable sales growth.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was approximately $53 million. Segment EBITDA margins for company-owned restaurants declined by about 220 basis points, mainly from labor inflation, aggregator fees, advertising, and higher food costs, partially offset by average ticket growth.
  • North America commissary adjusted EBITDA margins improved by 130 basis points to 7.3%.
  • Net cash provided by operating activities for the first six months was approximately $67 million, and free cash flow was $37 million.

Q&A

  • Tyler Prause, Stephens, asked about frequency trends linked to regional marketing efforts. Penegor responded that early tests are underway with franchisees and that "it's still the early innings of all of that."
  • Andrew Strelzik, BMO, inquired about traction from initiatives and confidence in the reacceleration of comps. Penegor cited new product launches and loyalty program growth as drivers, while acknowledging a cautious Q3 start due to a softer carryout business.
  • Sara Senatore, BofA, questioned four-wall economics and supply chain benefits. Penegor confirmed ongoing improvement and supply chain optimization, noting, "We're embarking and now executing a supply chain optimization project that can realize significant saves over time."
  • Christopher O'Cull, Stifel, explored marketing spend and supply chain changes. Penegor detailed the $9 million Q2 incremental marketing investment and plans for over $50 million in supply chain savings by 2028.
  • Peter Saleh, BTIG, and Penegor discussed loyalty program enhancements, with Penegor stating, "Since we enhanced our Papa Rewards program in November 2024, it's been nice to see that we've added 2.7 million new accounts."
  • Eric Gonzalez, KeyBanc, asked about innovation cadence. Penegor explained the pipeline was "relatively bare" but is now rebuilding, with several new products in the pipeline for late 2025 and 2026.
  • James Sanderson, Northcoast, sought updates on U.K. trends. Thanawala reported that U.K. comps accelerated in July, outperforming international trends, with strong consumer satisfaction and delivery times.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a generally positive tone, focusing on drivers of comp growth, innovation cadence, and marketing ROI. Questions reflected interest in the pace of transformation rather than skepticism or concern.
  • Management’s tone was confident in prepared remarks, emphasizing progress, but shifted to measured optimism in Q&A, particularly regarding carryout softness and innovation rollout, using phrases such as "early innings" and "we're watching in our carryout business."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed increased confidence, but discussions around supply chain and carryout signaled ongoing caution in select areas.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 saw positive North America comps of 1%, up from -2.7% in Q1, and international comps rising to 4% from 3%. Revenue growth accelerated to 4% in Q2 from 1% in Q1.
  • The company raised its international comparable sales guidance to 2%-4% for 2025, compared to the prior range of flat to 2%.
  • Management continued to stress innovation, marketing investment, and technology, but Q2 featured more tangible results from these initiatives, including a larger loyalty membership increase and new product launches.
  • Analyst questions in Q2 shifted more toward execution and future growth rather than stabilization and turnaround.
  • Confidence in achieving guidance and long-term transformation appeared higher, with more specific milestones and results shared.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted ongoing pressures on company-owned restaurant EBITDA margins from labor, aggregator fees, and food costs, particularly cheese and proteins.
  • Consumer caution and softer carryout business were cited as current headwinds, especially in early Q3.
  • Management is monitoring competitive promotional activity, particularly from larger rivals, and is prepared to "check and adjust" strategies as needed.
  • The company expects higher closure rates in both North America and international markets due to strategic optimization and underperforming locations, but indicated most closures have lower-than-average sales volume.
  • Supply chain optimization and refranchising are underway, but execution risks remain as these initiatives scale.

Final Takeaway

Papa John's management underscored tangible progress in its transformation strategy with a return to positive sales growth in North America, a raised outlook for international comparable sales, and ongoing investments in product innovation, marketing, technology, and supply chain optimization. The company remains focused on sustaining momentum through the second half of 2025 and beyond, while navigating cost pressures and evolving consumer trends with a disciplined, data-driven approach.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Apollo's stagflation alert: Inflation up, jobs down, and the Fed on the fence
data.description:
A fresh wave of stagflation concerns is sweeping across markets following the latest economic signals, Apollo chief economist Torsten Sløk highlighted.

In an investor note on Thursday, Sløk underscored that inflationary pressures within the U.S. services sector are accelerating, as evidenced by July’s ISM Services Prices Paid index. The data suggest that consumer price inflation could remain elevated in the months ahead. 

At the same time, labor market momentum is losing steam. Job growth is decelerating, and the unemployment rate is ticking higher—signs that the economy’s underlying strength is beginning to crack. 

Sløk attributes the current stagflationary trend to a mix of policy and macroeconomic shifts, including rising tariffs, increased deportations, and a weakening U.S. dollar, all of which are combining to drive prices higher while suppressing growth. 

This presents a complex dilemma for the Federal Reserve. Should policymakers prioritize inflation and tighten monetary policy, or respond to weakening growth with rate cuts? While markets continue to price in future rate reductions, Sløk warns that upside risks to inflation remain underappreciated. 

He advises investors to pay close attention to inflation expectations—both market-based and survey-driven—as the Fed navigates an increasingly treacherous economic landscape. 

Market Tracking ETFs: (NYSEARCA:DIA), (NYSEARCA:DDM), (NYSEARCA:DOG), (NYSEARCA:DXD), (NYSEARCA:SDOW), (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), (NYSEARCA:SPXU), (NASDAQ:QQQ), (NASDAQ:QQQM), (NASDAQ:TQQQ), (NYSEARCA:QID), and (NASDAQ:SQQQ).

data.title: Civitas targets $4.5B year-end net debt and accelerates $750M share buyback amid leadership change
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Civitas Resources (CIVI) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Wouter T. van Kempen, Interim CEO, announced the board's decision to part ways with Chris Doyle, emphasizing that "this is not a strategic shift for Civitas" but a move to deepen focus on "execution and performance on discipline and on cost leadership and push the company forward." He will serve as interim CEO while a permanent replacement is sought, and Howard Willard has been appointed Board Chair.
  • Van Kempen outlined four priorities for 2025: maximize free cash flow through cost leadership, target $4.5 billion in net debt by year-end, return cash to shareholders primarily through a strong base dividend and potential for higher returns upon hitting debt targets, and continue ESG initiatives.
  • Marianella Foschi, CFO, highlighted actions to strengthen the company, including optimizing investment levels, increasing oil hedges to "approximately 60%...for the remainder of this year, which is about twice our normal levels," issuing $750 million in new senior notes to enhance liquidity, and exceeding the full-year target for noncore asset sales by executing agreements to divest $435 million in DJ Basin assets.
  • Foschi stated, "With confidence in our plan, we intend to take advantage of the compelling value our equity provides today" and described a "$750 million" share repurchase authorization, representing "about 28% of our current market cap." An accelerated share repurchase program of $250 million is expected to be completed in the third quarter.
  • President and COO Clayton A. Carrell noted strong operational momentum, with efficiencies in the Permian leading to "drilled footage per day...around 20% higher than planned" and initial Delaware pad wells in New Mexico averaging over 1,200 barrels of oil per day. In the DJ Basin, Carrell cited drilling and completion efficiencies, with "4-mile laterals spud to spud in about 6 days."

Outlook

  • Full-year volume guidance has been updated to reflect asset divestitures, with second-half production expected to grow approximately 7% and third quarter production anticipated to be higher than the fourth due to divestiture timing.
  • The company plans to allocate 50% of free cash flow after the base dividend to share buybacks for full year 2025, amounting to about $375 million in repurchases, including $70 million already completed.
  • Cash operating costs for the second half are expected to average less than $10 per BOE, and full-year capital expenditure outlook remains on track.
  • Management reiterated confidence in meeting the $4.5 billion year-end net debt target and indicated that future free cash flow will continue to be allocated between debt reduction and shareholder returns.

Financial Results

  • Oil volumes increased 6% quarter-over-quarter.
  • Cash operating expenses on a unit basis were more than 10% lower compared to the previous quarter, with capital investments at the low end of plan.
  • The quarter delivered nearly $750 million in adjusted EBITDA and over $120 million in adjusted free cash flow.
  • Well costs were reported as 7% lower in the Delaware, 5% lower in the Midland, and 3% lower in the DJ compared to the beginning of the year. The company has achieved about 80% of its $100 million cost optimization target for 2025.
  • Asset divestitures of $435 million were executed at a 4x multiple on 2026 cash flow, with production from divested assets estimated at around 10,000 barrels equivalent per day next year.

Q&A

  • Benjamin Zachary Parham, JPMorgan: Questioned the timing and rationale behind resuming aggressive buybacks given current net debt. Foschi responded that the company is "in a more advantaged position today because of the recent steps we've taken, including the incremental hedges, the cost optimization program and the divestments along with the debt term out."
  • Parham followed up on 2026 production plans post-asset sale. Carrell indicated maintenance capital would hold oil production in the "145 to 150" range for 2026, reflecting impacts from asset divestitures.
  • Phillip Jungwirth, BMO: Asked Carrell for operational impressions and areas for improvement. Carrell described ongoing efficiency gains and cost reductions, especially in the DJ and facility fronts.
  • Jungwirth sought details on asset sales and potential future divestitures. Foschi said, "We were patient...these were assets that were further down the development plan for us...minimal to no near-term impact."
  • Scott Michael Hanold, RBC: Raised concerns about debt reduction versus buybacks. Foschi stated, "Having clarity on 4.5 was the first and foremost goal...we continue to believe that we need to maintain a strong capital structure."
  • Francis Lloyd Byrne, Jefferies: Inquired about dividend levels. Foschi affirmed commitment to the base dividend and noted that "buying 10% of our market cap...will interimly save about $20 million to $25 million in dividends on a go-forward basis."
  • Multiple analysts probed cost optimization timelines and operational consistency across basins. Carrell highlighted progress, with "80% captured on the $100 million run rate in 2026" and ongoing initiatives to further reduce costs.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on debt reduction, capital returns, and sustainability of operational improvements, with a slightly skeptical tone regarding the timing of buybacks versus further deleveraging.
  • Management maintained a confident, proactive tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently stressing cost discipline and operational execution. van Kempen stated, "We have a high level of confidence in our go-forward plan."
  • Compared to last quarter, management’s tone is more assertive about execution and returning capital, while analysts remain cautious about the pace of buybacks amid outstanding debt.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The transition from Chris Doyle to van Kempen as interim CEO marks a notable leadership change, explicitly framed as a move to deepen focus on execution rather than a strategic overhaul.
  • Guidance language has shifted to emphasize accelerated capital returns and buybacks, supported by divestiture proceeds and operational efficiencies.
  • Analysts in both quarters pressed on debt targets, but this quarter saw increased focus on balancing buybacks with further deleveraging.
  • Compared to Q1, management is more vocal about completed asset sales, higher hedging levels, and cost reduction progress, while analysts’ skepticism around capital allocation persists.
  • The company’s confidence in meeting its $4.5 billion net debt target by year-end remains unchanged, but messaging around capital returns is more prominent.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged macro volatility and commodity price uncertainty, but cited increased hedging and liquidity as mitigants.
  • Analysts repeatedly questioned the prudence of prioritizing share buybacks over deeper debt reduction, with management reiterating the importance of maintaining a robust capital structure and flexibility.
  • Asset divestiture proceeds and hedging gains are critical to achieving stated targets; delays or pricing changes could impact guidance.

Final Takeaway

Civitas Resources outlined decisive steps in Q2 2025, including a leadership transition, accelerated asset sales, and enhanced capital return programs. Management reiterated confidence in achieving its $4.5 billion net debt target by year-end, supported by strong operational efficiencies, lower costs, and robust hedging. The company now plans to allocate 50% of free cash flow after the base dividend to share buybacks, with $375 million targeted for 2025 and a $750 million authorization in place. Despite analyst concerns about the balance between buybacks and debt reduction, Civitas remains focused on disciplined execution, cost leadership, and delivering increased shareholder value through a clear and consistent strategy.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Hecla Mining jumps to YTD high after easy earnings beat, quarterly record free cash flow
data.description:
Hecla Mining (NYSE:HL) +16.8% in Thursday's trading to its highest since October after easily beating expectations for Q2 earnings and revenues while posting quarterly record free cash flow, driven by strong silver and gold production and standout performances at its Keno Hill and Lucky Friday mines.

Hecla (NYSE:HL) said it generated record sales of $304M, record free cash flow of $103.8M, and record adjusted EBITDA of $132.5M, while improving net leverage to 0.7x, slashed from 1.5x in Q1 due in part to the partial redemption of $212M in 7.25% senior notes and full repayment of C$50M IQ notes.

The move will free up $17.8M/year in interest expense, "allowing us to refocus those funds towards strengthening our balance sheet while enabling strategic reinvestment into the highest return opportunities," Hecla (HL) President and CEO Rob Krcmarov said.

Q2 silver production surged 10% Y/Y to 4.52M oz while gold output jumped 34% to 45.9K oz; Q2 silver sales were flat Q/Q due mostly to concentrate inventory build at Greens Creek, while gold sales rose primarily due to a strong operational quarter at Casa Berardi.

Hecla (HL) said the Lucky Friday mine set a new quarterly milling record with nearly 114.5K processed, exceeding the previous record by 5%, and Keno Hill marked a milestone by achieving its first quarter of positive free cash flow.

Q2 silver all-in sustained cost fell to $5.19/oz of silver, primarily due to higher silver production, lower treatment charges, and higher by-product credits from higher gold production and realized gold prices.

data.title: Stocks cut gains, trade mixed as investors weigh Trump tariffs, Russia-Ukraine progress
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages on Thursday gave up a positive open to trade mixed as a boost to sentiment from U.S. President Donald Trump's chip tariff exemptions and progress on Russia-Ukraine de-escalation petered out.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) turned negative in morning trade, giving up an advance of as much as +0.7%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) was last -0.6%. 

The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.5%, boosted by a post-earnings climb in AppLovin (APP) and DoorDash (DASH), and extended gains in tech titan Apple (AAPL). The iPhone-maker on Wednesday confirmed a new $100B investment in the U.S.

The tech-heavy index was also boosted by several chip stocks, as investors took heart from Trump's vow to exempt semiconductor manufacturers from a planned 100% tariff on imports if they made their products in the United States.

President Trump “won’t charge tariffs while you’re building in the country, but if you’re not building in the country, 100% tariffs,” Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick told Fox Business in the morning.

Shares of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), the world's largest contract chipmaker, clinched a record high on Thursday after Taiwan said TSM will not have to pay the 100% tariff.

Speaking of tariffs, reciprocal barriers that had been postponed twice finally went into effect against U.S. trading partners across the world on Wednesday, bringing the average tariff rate to levels not seen since World War II.   

“Is this the new global tariff regime? Not quite yet. While some countries are still negotiating with the U.S., America’s new tariff regime entered into effect at midnight, increasing the effective U.S. tariff level from just under 3% at the beginning of the year to just over 15%,” said Mohamed A. El-Erian, chief economic adviser at Allianz and former CEO of PIMCO.

“Up to this point, many companies appear to have used inventories and profit margins as buffers, as well as pressure on suppliers, while waiting to see what would finally emerge. Now, they must make more challenging, longer-term decisions about whether and how much to adjust prices,” El-Erian added. 

Also grabbing headlines were signs of progress on a truce between Russia and Ukraine.

"We had some very good talks with President Putin today and there's a very good chance that we could be ending the round — the end of that road. There's a good chance that there will be a meeting very soon," Trump told reporters on Wednesday.

Meanwhile, The New York Times reported that Trump was planning to hold in-person meetings with Russian and Ukranian leaders Vladimir Putin and Volodymyr Zelenskyy as soon as next week. 

Turning to Thursday's economic calendar, initial jobless claims jumped more than expected, while continuing claims rose to their highest mark since 2021.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was little changed at 4.23%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) was up 1 basis point to 3.73%. Traders will be keeping an eye on an upcoming $25B 30-year Treasury bond auction.
